coredata.dc:title: Xylan derived fluorescence carbon dots composite with cotton cellulose paper as ‘turn-off’ fluorescence platform for sensitive and selective detection Cu2+ in real samples
coredata.dc:creator: Zhang, Yingying
Feng, Xiuyuan
Chen, Zhiyuan
Cui, Xiaoci
Xiao, Huining
Xiong, Ranhua
Huang, Chaobo
coredata.dc:description: The pollution of heavy metals such as Cu2+ is still serious and the discharge of sewage of Cu2+ will cause damage to soil environment and human health. Herein, a biomass-based solid-state fluorescence detection platform (CPU-CDs) was developed as fluorescent sensor for detection Cu2+ via fluorescence and colorimetric dual-model methods in real time. CPU-CDs was composed of xylan-derived CDs (U-CDs) and cotton cellulose paper, which exhibiting good reusability, non-toxicity, excellent fluorescence characteristics and high biocompatibility. Further, CPU-CDs displayed high effectiveness and sensitivity for Cu2+ with the detection limit as low as 0.14 μM, which was well below U.S. EPA safety levels (20 μM). Practical application indicated that CPU-CDs could achieve precision response of Cu2+ change in real environment water samples with good recovery range of 90 %–119 %. This strategy demonstrated a promising biomass solid-state fluorescence sensor for Cu2+ detection for water treatment research, which is of great significance in dealing with water pollution caused by heavy metal ions.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Xylan derived carbon dots
Fluorescent sensors
Cu2+ detection

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: CPU-CDs for real-time monitoring of Cu2+ in zebrafish
Fabrication and microstructural characteristics of CPU-CDs
Fluorescence behavior of CPU-CDs for detecting Cu2+
Biocompatibility of U-CDs and fluorescence imaging of CPU-CDs

xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency-matched-string: Postgraduate Research & Practice Innovation Program of Jiangsu Province
National Natural Science Foundation of China

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Schematic diagram of the conduction process and application of U-CDs and CPU-CDs, respectively.

(A) Schematic illustration the preparation xylan-derived CDs (U-CDs) with blue emission. (B) FTIR spectra of xylan power. (C) FTIR spectra of U-CDs. (D) Digital images of U-CDs under daylight and UV–vis light (365 nm). (E) XRD spectra of U-CDs. (F) TEM image and size distribution of U-CDs. (G) HRTEM image of U-CDs. (H) AFM image of U-CDs. (IK) High-resolution XPS of C1s, N1s and O1s spectra of U-CDs.

(A) Schematic illustration the preparation of CPU-CDs. (B) CPU-CDs with and without 365 nm ultraviolet light. SEM (20 μm) and FT-IR of CPU-CDs. (C) Binding mechanism of U-CDs with the filter paper derived from cotton fibers. (D) Different concentration of CPU-CDs with Cu2+. (E) Time stability of CPU-CDs with and without Cu2+.

(A) Excitation characteristic of U-CDs. (B) UV–vis spectrum of CPU-CDs. (C) Fluorescent intensity changes of CPU-CDs under ions including K+, Ca2+, Na+, Mg2+, Zn2+, Fe2+, Ni+, Hg2+, Al3+, Fe3+, Cu2+, Cr3+, Ni2+, F−, CN−, ACO−, HPO4 2−, NO2 −, SCN− and HS−. λ ex = 310 nm. (D) The fluorescence emission spectra change of CPU-CDs with different concentrations of Cu2+. λ ex = 310 nm. (E) Fluorescence intensity change of CPU-CDs + Cu2+after mixed with different ions including K+, Ca2+, Na+, Mg2+, Zn2+, Fe2+, Ni+, Hg2+, Al3+, Fe3+, Cr3+, Ni2+, F−, CN−, ACO−, HPO4 2−, NO2 −, SCN− and HS−. (F) Time stability of CPU-CDs in the experiment time of 8 d. (G) Binding mechanism of CPU-CDs + Cu2+.

(A) Images showed the co-culture of U-CDs and CPU-CDs in mung bean and zebrafish embryos. (B) Digital images of U-CDs and mung bean co-cultured for 12 days. (C) The normalized length of mung bean root length changed within 12 days of culture cycle. (D) Fluorescence images of CPU-CDs and CPU-CDs + Cu2+ in zebrafish embryos under blue channel, scale bar: 20 um. (E) The normalized fluorescence intensity of CPU-CDs and CPU-CDs + Cu2+ in zebrafish embryos. (F) Fluorescence images of CPU-CDs and CPU-CDs + Cu2+ in zebrafish embryos under different concentrations under blue channel, scale bar: 20 um. (G) The normalized fluorescence intensity of CPU-CDs and CPU-CDs + Cu2+ in zebrafish embryos under different concentrations.

(A) Schematic illustration of preparation of biomass fluorescence ink by using U-CDs. (B) Digital images of biomass fluorescence ink under daylight and sunlight. (C) Water resistance of the fluorescent ink. (D) Stability of the fluorescent ink at room temperature. (E) Practical application of fluorescence ink.
Recovery rates of Cu2+ in real water samples.

head.ce:title: Xylan derived fluorescence carbon dots composite with cotton cellulose paper as ‘turn-off’ fluorescence platform for sensitive and selective detection Cu2+ in real samples

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Joint Laboratory of Advanced Biomedical Materials (NFU-UGent), Jiangsu Co-Innovation Center of Efficient Processing and Utilization of Forest Resources, College of Chemical Engineering, Nanjing Forestry University (NFU), Nanjing 210037, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Joint Laboratory of Advanced Biomedical Materials (NFU-UGent)
Jiangsu Co-Innovation Center of Efficient Processing and Utilization of Forest Resources

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Joint Laboratory of Advanced Biomedical Materials (NFU-UGent), Jiangsu Co-Innovation Center of Efficient Processing and Utilization of Forest Resources, College of Chemical Engineering, Nanjing Forestry University (NFU), Nanjing 210037, China

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Chemical Engineering, University of New Brunswick, Fredericton, New Brunswick E3B5A3, Canada
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Department of Chemical Engineering, University of New Brunswick, Fredericton, New Brunswick, E3B5A3, Canada

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: The pollution of heavy metals such as Cu2+ is still serious and the discharge of sewage of Cu2+ will cause damage to soil environment and human health. Herein, a biomass-based solid-state fluorescence detection platform (CPU-CDs) was developed as fluorescent sensor for detection Cu2+ via fluorescence and colorimetric dual-model methods in real time. CPU-CDs was composed of xylan-derived CDs (U-CDs) and cotton cellulose paper, which exhibiting good reusability, non-toxicity, excellent fluorescence characteristics and high biocompatibility. Further, CPU-CDs displayed high effectiveness and sensitivity for Cu2+ with the detection limit as low as 0.14 μM, which was well below U.S. EPA safety levels (20 μM). Practical application indicated that CPU-CDs could achieve precision response of Cu2+ change in real environment water samples with good recovery range of 90 %–119 %. This strategy demonstrated a promising biomass solid-state fluorescence sensor for Cu2+ detection for water treatment research, which is of great significance in dealing with water pollution caused by heavy metal ions.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: No data was used for the research described in the article.

ce:section.ce:para: Among various heavy metal metals, copper (Cu2+) is one of the most harmful chemical species to environment and living organisms [1–6]. The Cu2+ mining, smelting, electroplating and electronics industries discharge a large amount of Cu2+ containing wastewater every year [7–9]. The discharge of heavy metal Cu2+ poses great harm to water and soil. Cu2+ is one of the important trace elements, but if Cu2+ content exceeds the standard in human body, it will burden human organ, especially liver and gallbladder [10,11]. And the World Health Organization (WHO) and Environmental Protection Agency (EPA) have set the current guideline values of Cu2+ in drinking water at 2 mg/L (∼30 μM) and 1.3 mg/L (∼20 μM), respectively [12]. Hence, there is an urgent need to develop a strategy to detect changes in Cu2+.

Currently, electrochemical [13,14], spectrophotometric analysis (i.e. atomic absorption spectroscopy, X-ray fluorescence spectrometry and so on) [15], petroleum-based fluorescence probes (i.e. naphthalene derivatives, [5]helicene, diethyl aminocoumarin and so on) [16–19] have been used for detection Cu2+. Those methods show high sensitivity and rapid response among detection process [20–23]. However, they usually suffer from expensive and cumbersome instrument, skill-man conduction and complicated pretreatment process, which restrict their practical application especially for fast and in situ detection of Cu2+.

Importantly, fluorescence carbon dots (CDs) have shown more applications in fluorescence detection, anti-counterfeiting printing and bioimaging due to their good fluorescent luminous effect, easy operation and simple preparation [24–27]. The application of biomass derived CDs used for metal ions detection is gradually extensive, which showing the following advantages [28–31]. Initially, the precursors of synthetic biomass CDs are basically renewable (i.e., fruits, vegetables and trees) and some of them are even industrial wastes [32,33]. Further, biomass materials contain other elements except carbon, hydrogen and oxygen, so the synthesis of CDs doped with different elements can be realized without adding additional heteroatomic sources [29,34]. Importantly, biomass derived CDs usually display good biocompatibility and low toxicity [27,35]. Besides, biomass CDs usually has many groups on its surface, good water solubility and easy functionalization, which provides infinite possibilities for further modification of materials [30,36–39]. Therefore, with the in-depth study of biomass CDs, it will broaden the field of fluorescence sensing.

It was notably that xylan is a kind of rich woody biomass resources and is the main component of hemicellulose, usually shows advantages of non-toxic and harmless, renewable, biodegradable and hydrophilicity, which provides a broad application prospect for its high-value utilization [40–42]. Xylan is composed of three elements, C, H and O, and the content of C is >40 %, which can ensure the formation of the main carbon skeleton structure of CDs in nucleation process [43,44]. On the other hand, the oxygen-containing groups of xylan molecules also create conditions for formation of fluorescence emission potential well of CDs and provide heteroatomic doping sites [45]. Therefore, taking xylan as a kind of green and renewable carbon resources and constructing it into functional composite CDs materials has certain ecological and economic benefits and facilitating the realization of ‘dual‑carbon’ goals.

Herein, a simple strategy to fabricate a novel biomass based solid-state fluorescent platform (CPU-CDs) with effective, visual, reusable, inexpensive and highly sensitive characteristics was proposed for detecting Cu2+ (Fig. 1 ). Fluorescence CDs (U-CDs) was synthesized by using xylan as carbon source and urea as a dopant. Considering the secondary pollution of U-CDs powder and the difficulty of storage and leakage of liquid, it is proposed that the rationality of promoting the synthesis of CPU-CDs through the electrostatic adsorption and chemical crosslinking between the functional groups of fluorescence CDs and cotton cellulose paper. Further, the spectra and detection behavior of CPU-CDs as fluorescence sensor for portable, synergistic enhanced in-situ, real-time and rapid detection of Cu2+ were investigated. This strategy provides a reference for exploring the preparation of a variety of biomass fluorescence sensing materials for detection Cu2+ and also opens up a new application scenario for the high value-added utilization of xylan.

Beechwood xylan (95 %), urea (99 %), ethanol (95 %), Polyvinylpyrrolidone (PVP, Mw = 5800), CMC-Na (1500–3100 Mpa.s) was obtained from Shanghai Maclin Biochemical Technology Co., LTD, respectively. Anhydrous ethanol (EtOH, 95 %) was purchased from offline reagent stores. Cotton cellulose paper was purchased from Taobao store. Zebrafish embryos (male, 6 days) were purchased from Shanghai Biotechnology Co., LTD. Portable UV–vis lamp (365 nm) was purchased from local shop.
Details of experimental characterization instruments can be found in Supporting information.

Xylan and NaOH solution (mass concentration 2 %) were mixed well and stirred at room temperature. Then, excess anhydrous ethanol solution was added into above solution. When xylan precipitated completely and white flocculent precipitated and then filtered by a drainage PTFE needle filter (0.22 μm), and then excessive anhydrous ethanol was added for repeated washing and precipitation. The obtained precipitation was dried to constant weight at 40 °C to obtain xylan with high purity.

The purified xylan (0.1 g) was putted into a beaker with 30 mL deionized water, and urea (0.3 g) was added slowly under continuously stirred at 50 °C for 1 h. Finally, the mixed solution was transferred to a hydrothermal reactor (50 mL) and heated at 180 °C for 12 h. Above mixtures were cooled to 25 °C and separated with a pinhole filter (0.22 μm). The filtrate was collected into a cellulose dialysis bag (500 Da) and was dialyzed with deionized water for 48 h. Collected the solution and dried in vacuum oven at 40 °C until the weight was constant to obtain the solid sample powder of U-CDs.
Cotton cellulose paper (with certain size) was placed into petri dish containing U-CDs (20 mL), soaked in vacuum at room temperature for 1 h, and taken out to dry before use.

Various analytes including K+, Ca2+, Na+, Mg2+, Zn2+, Fe2+, Cu2+, Ni+, Hg2+, Al3+, Fe3+, Cr3+, Ni2+, F−, CN−, ACO−, HPO4 2−, NO2 −, SCN− and HS− were prepared, respectively. Concentration of above analytes kept at 25 μM. CPU-CDs was exposed to various substances to evaluate its selectivity by using fluorescence emission spectrum (silt = 4 nm, λ ex = 310 nm). In addition, CPU-CDs was infiltrated by other metal ions and then infiltrated into Cu2+ solution to study its anti-interference detection effect.

Fluorescent ink was composed of main agent and auxiliary agent. The main agent was fluorescent material and connecting material, which was the main factor that determines the performance of fluorescent ink. The auxiliaries were adhesive, stabilizer and surfactant. In this study, U-CDs dispersion was used as fluorescent material, ethanol as bonding material, sodium carboxymethyl cellulose as binder and stabilizer and PVP as surfactant.

ce:section.ce:section-title: CPU-CDs for real-time monitoring of Cu2+ in zebrafish
ce:section.ce:para: Cu2+ in zebrafish samples were detected according to previous method [46], and the relevant operation methods were described in detail in the supporting literature.
Methods can be found in Supporting information.
Methods can be found in Supporting information.

Herein, taking hemicellulose main ingredients xylan as carbon source material, urea as a nitrogen dopant, then biomass CDs was synthesized by using ‘one step’ rapid hydrothermal synthesis method, namely U-CDs (Fig. 2A). Xylan surface was rich in hydroxyl group, making it a selective sensor for Cu2+ sensing. The property of U-CDs was characterized by FT-IR, XRD, XPS, TEM, HRTEM and AFM, as shown in Fig. 2. Initially, as shown in Fig. 2B, the peak showed at 3340 cm−1 in the FT-IR spectrum of xylan belonged to the stretching vibration of OH. The absorption peak near 2925 cm−1 corresponded to the stretching vibration of the CH bond. And CO and CC caused by the stretching vibration absorption peaks appeared around 1703 cm−1 and 1635 cm−1, respectively. The peak at 1353 cm−1 was the in-plane bending vibration of phenolic hydroxyl groups. The peak at 1250–1000 cm−1 was related to the stretching vibration of C-O-H and C-O-C in xylan molecular. On the other side, FT-IR method showed that U-CDs has a strong infrared absorption band near 3415 cm−1, indicating that chemical composition contains abundant hydroxyl groups (Fig. 2C). Besides, the peak near 3247 cm−1 indicated that the surface of U-CDs contained a small amount of −NH2 groups. And absorption peak of 2925 cm−1 was attributed to the stretching vibration of C − H. The wide absorption peak near 1600 cm−1 corresponded to stretching vibration of CC and bending vibration of N − H, and the absorption peak near 1460 cm−1 was related to stretching vibration of C − N. The characteristic absorption peak at 1068 cm−1 was the C − O stretching vibration absorption peak. FT-IR analysis showed that U-CDs showed a clear infrared absorption peak related to N, indicating that CH₄N₂O was used as dopant nitrogen atom doping of CDs can be achieved effectively. Besides, the fluorescence quantum of U-CDs yield was calculated as 36 %. Under sunlight, the color of U-CDs was light brown liquid. And after UV–vis lamp irradiation (365 nm), U-CDs showed bright blue fluorescence signal, which proved that U-CDs have excellent fluorescence effect, as shown in Fig. 2D inset. Further, XRD analysis of U-CDs showed a wide diffraction peak near 2θ = 20° (Fig. 2E), indicating that U-CDs has an amorphous carbon core structure. TEM in Fig. 2F shown that the spherical particles were monodispersed at room temperature and did not agglomerate when the U-CDs concentration was as high as 0.5 mg/mL. The size of U-CDs solution ranged from 1.5 nm to 3.0 nm, indicating that U-CDs has a wide particle size distribution range with average size about 2.40 nm. Fig. 2G showed the HRTEM analysis results of U-CDs. It can be observed that U-CDs has significant lattice fringes. According to the selection diffraction results, the spacing between the graphitized carbon core layers was 0.21 nm. Further, AFM images of U-CDs showed that the height of U-CDs was mainly distributed in 3.5 nm, indicating that U-CDs was mainly composed of a single graphite layer with outstanding quantum confinement effect [47]. The main element composition of U-CDs was analyzed by XPS, as shown in Fig. 2I-K. Fig. 2I showed the XPS high-resolution spectrum of C1s. The analysis showed that the main forms of C element in U-CDs were C − C/C=C, C − N, C − O, CO which attributed to the 284.5 eV, 285.3 eV, 286.3 eV and 288.6 eV, respectively. Among them, the existence of CC bond reflected the large number of sp2 hybrid carbon atoms in the structure of U-CDs. The N1s map in Fig. 2J showed XPS peaks of 399.4 eV and 400.8 eV, corresponding to pyrrole N and graphite N, respectively. The comparison of peak area of N1s spectra displayed that pyrrole N is the main form of N element in the structure composition of U-CDs. As an electron donor, pyrrole N can increase the electron cloud density on CDs surface, which was conducive to exciton radiation. Fig. 2K showed binding energies of O1s peaks of 532.7 eV and 531.6 eV can be attributed to C − OH/C − O − C and CO, respectively. According to above results, indicating U-CDs has a large number of N/O functional groups.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Fabrication and microstructural characteristics of CPU-CDs
ce:section.ce:para: It worth note that the use of CDs solution detection presents several issues, including poor portability, susceptibility to light, heat decomposition and unstable detection. Herein, cotton cellulose paper with low cost and wide source was selected as the solid matrix material and fluorescence U-CDs were used to adhered to cotton cellulose paper by electrostatic adsorption, as shown in Fig. 3A and C. As shown in Fig. 3B, under ultraviolet lamp (365 nm), the solid-state CPU-CDs displayed bright blue fluorescence signal, which proved that it has good photoluminescence effect and can be further used for metal ion detection. SEM analysis proved the porous structure of CPU-CDs, which provided potential for subsequent adsorption and detection of Cu2+. The characteristic absorption peak appeared after U-CDs impregnated with cotton cellulose paper, characteristic peak at 3415 cm−1 was related to the -OH group. Compared to that at 3459 cm−1 of U-CDs (Fig. 2C), this peak at 3415 cm−1 was blue-shifted. Besides, a peak at 1600 cm−1 was reduced in the CPU-CDs attributed to the stretching vibration of the CN. Then, CPU-CDs detection Cu2+ was further conducted by using naked-eve view. As shown in Fig. 3D, the color of CPU-CDs was light blue under a UV–vis lamp (365 nm). In addition, with Cu2+ concentration increasing, the fluorescence of CPU-CDs gradually weakens, until the concentration of Cu2+ reached at 50 μM, the fluorescence signal appeared completely quenched. It can be seen that CPU-CDs shows effective and highly sensitive characteristics for Cu2+. In addition, within the test time of 52 h the bright blue fluorescence of CPU-CDs did not significantly weaken. Moreover, the fluorescence of CPU-CDs remained in quenching state after combined with Cu2+ (40 μM), as shown in Fig. 3E. The above results proved that the CPU-CDs has excellent stability for Cu2+ detection.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Fluorescence behavior of CPU-CDs for detecting Cu2+
ce:section.ce:para: In order to reveal the fluorescence emission behavior of U-CDs, fluorescence emission spectra of U-CDs were investigated. As excitation wavelength of U-CDs increased from 300 nm to 480 nm, the maximum fluorescence emission wavelength at 435 nm of U-CDs gradually redshifted from 410 nm to 560 nm. The fluorescence emission behavior depending on the excitation wavelength may be related to the size effect (quantum limited effect) of U-CDs or different surface defect potential wells, as shown in Fig. 4A. The UV–vis spectrum of Fig. 4B showed that CPU-CDs has maximum ultraviolet excitation wavelength at 310 nm. The fluorescence intensity changes of CPU-CDs after mixed with different substances was investigated as shown in Fig. 4C. CPU-CDs had the maximum fluorescence intensity at 435 nm. When other analytes (K+, Ca2+, Na+, Mg2+, Zn2+, Fe2+, Ni+, Hg2+, Al3+, Fe3+, Cr3+, Ni2+, F−, CN−, ACO−, HPO4 2−, NO2 −, SCN− and HS−) were blended with the CPU-CDs extract, the fluorescence intensity of CPU-CDs was not enhanced and quenched significantly. However, when Cu2+ were added to the CPU-CDs solution, the fluorescence intensity of the CPU-CDs was obviously quenched, which directly indicated the unique selectivity and high sensitivity of the membrane to Cu2+. To further investigated the behavior of CPU-CDs for Cu2+, different concentrations Cu2+ (0–300 μM) affect the fluorescence intensity change of CPU-CDs was investigated. With Cu2+ concentration increasing, the maximum fluorescence intensity at 435 nm decreased gradually and gradually red-shifted to 484 nm. The change of Cu2+ concentration and fluorescence intensity were linearly fitted in Fig. 4D, the curve showed a good linear relationship in the range of 15 μM to 75 μM with I 484 nm/I 435 nm = 0.157–1.32[Cu2+] (R2 = 0.997). The detection limit of CPU-CDs for Cu2+ was calculated as 0.14 μM. The values were lower than the reported Cu2+ fluorescence sensors, indicating the good sensitively of CPU-CDs for Cu2+ (Table 1 ). Further proved that CPU-CDs could be used to detect Cu2+ via fluorescence spectra and quantitatively calculated. Furthermore, anti-interference ability and competition experiment of CPU-CDs for Cu2+ detection was conducted. Various ions including were cultured with CPU-CDs + Cu2+, then fluorescence intensity changed result were recorded in Fig. 4E. Above results displaying the CPU-CDs has good anti-interference ability and can select Cu2+ specifically. Besides, CPU-CDs also showed high time-stability during 8d, which proved CPU-CDs can be used for longtime for Cu2+ detection (Fig. 4F). And showed good fluorescence stability in various solvent, as shown in Fig. S1. Further, the possible detection mechanisms of Cu2+ with CPU-CDs was inferred. Cotton cellulose paper contains a large number of hydroxyl groups, and after modifying U-CDs, the CPU-CDs surface has amino groups, amino groups can increase the trapping ability with Cu2+. As shown in Fig. 4G, solid fluorescence platform CPU-CDs can be combined with Cu2+ through covalent binding and electrostatic adsorption, so as to realize real-time detection of Cu2+.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Biocompatibility of U-CDs and fluorescence imaging of CPU-CDs
ce:section.ce:para: Since the raw materials of U-CDs were derived from biomass-based materials, they have good biocompatibility and safety. U-CDs solution and CPU-CDs extract solution as culture substrate to culture mung bean and zebrafish embryos, and observed the growth condition of mung bean, as shown in Fig. 5(A). From Fig. 5(B), when the mung bean was cultured with U-CDs solution, the mung bean gradually germinated, took root and gave birth to leaves. The results showed that the U-CDs solution has higher safety for plants. Fig. 5(C) showed the change of mung bean root length within 12 days of the culture cycle. The results showed that compared with the control group (water), mung bean also had better growth and development. Then the compatibility and fluorescence imaging performance of the CPU-CDs with zebrafish embryos were studied, as shown in Fig. 5(C). Zebrafish embryos showed bright green fluorescence when CPU-CDs were cultured together with zebrafish embryos, which proved that CPU-CDs had better permeability and imaging ability to animal tissues. The potential of CPU-CDs detecting Cu2+ in zebrafish embryo was also further evaluated, and results showed that green fluorescence of zebrafish embryos was significantly quenched after co-cultured with Cu2+, as shown in Fig. 5(D, E). The results show that CPU-CDs was highly selective to Cu2+ in zebrafish embryo. Finally, CPU-CDs and CPU-CDs + Cu2+ were aslo shown concentration-dependent in zebrafish embryos, further proving CPU-CDs has good potential for detecting Cu2+ in living animals.

Water pollution caused by excessive discharge of heavy metal Cu2+ has attracted extensive attention from researchers in recent years [1]. Then, the potential of CPU-CDs for detection Cu2+ in water samples including school lake, Xuanwu Lake and Yangtze River were also explored. For proving the detection accuracy of CPU-CDs with Cu2+, the concentrations of Cu2+ were calculated according to fluorescence titration curve of CPU-CDs with Cu2+. As shown in Table 2 , the recovery rates of Cu2+ was 90 %–119 % in water samples. Above results revealed that CPU-CDs could quantitatively detect Cu2+ in real water environment with quantitative method.

In order to realize the high-value application of xylan derived U-CDs, its application potential as fluorescent ink was further explored. Fig. 6A showed the preparation method of U-CDs derived fluorescent ink. The dispersion of fluorescent ink is yellowish-white viscous liquid under sunlight. Under Uv-vis light (365 nm), it showed bright blue fluorescence, which proved the successful preparation of U-CDs ink and its potential as fluorescent ink, as shown in Fig. 6B. Further, the fluorescence ink to write ‘NJFU’ on cotton cellulose paper to prove its writing ability as ink was used. It's surprised to find that after writing, the filter paper has no obvious traces, but under Uv-vis light (365 nm), the ink presented a bright ‘NJFU’ signal. The results showed that the fluorescent ink has better bonding ability and higher concealability, can be used for fluorescence anti-counterfeiting related applications. Then the water resistance of fluorescent ink was studied, and the results showed that the fluorescent ink still had a bright fluorescent signal and stability within 30 days of the test, as shown in Fig. 6C. It is worth noting that the fluorescent ink stored at room temperature for 120 days still has high concealment and strong fluorescence signal, as shown in Fig. 6D. Finally, fluorescent ink was used to prepare the two-dimensional code. Under Uv-vis light (365 nm), two-dimensional code showed obvious blue fluorescence, and, can be decoded as ‘NJFU’ by mobile phone scanning. Above results proved that fluorescent ink has good concealability, can be used in fluorescence anti-counterfeiting and other fields, as shown in Fig. 6E.

The solution used as fluorescent probe for the detection of heavy metal ions has the disadvantages of easy leakage, difficult storage and recovery. Therefore, it is urgent to develop a solid-state fluorescence platform for real-time detection of heavy metal ion changes. Herein, using xylan as carbon source, nitrogen-doped fluorescent U-CDs and composited them with cotton cellulose paper by simple impregnation method to prepare a novel solid-state fluorescence sensor platform (CPU-CDs) was prepared. CPU-CDs realizing in situ, simple, real-time and visual fluorescence detection Cu2+ with detection limit is 0.14 μM, rapid response time (<1 s), good reliability in real water samples (90 %–119 %). The above results verify feasibility of CPU-CDs can be used as fluorescence sensor for in-situ detection of Cu2+ by using colorimetric and fluorescence dual model analysis. Further, U-CDs and CPU-CDs also depicted good fluorescence performance, high biocompatibility and safety in plants and living zebrafish, respectively. This method offers a portable tool for detection of Cu2+ in-situ, shows its potential application in monitoring water environment. Besides, U-CDs was also used as fluorescent ink and explored its potential in the fluorescence anti-counterfeiting domain. Overall, this work provides useful information for conducting multifunctional biomass materials, which can be used for analytes detection and fluorescence anti-counterfeiting.

Yingying Zhang designed and wrote the manuscript. Xiuyuan Feng, Zhiyuan Chen, and Xiaoci Cui performed the experiments. Huining Xiao provided the fluorescence equipment. Ranhua Xiong and Chaobo Huang revised the manuscript. All authors discussed the experiments and results. The authors have given approval to the final version of the manuscript.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no competing interests.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was financially supported by National Natural Science Foundation of China (No. 22275094 and No. 22275093) and Postgraduate Research & Practice Innovation Program of Jiangsu Province (No. KYCX23_1159).

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.ijbiomac.2023.127707.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Advancing water footprint assessments: combining the impacts of water pollution and scarcity
Sci. Total Environ.

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: E. Pierrat, A. Laurent, M. Dorber, M. Rygaard, F. Verones, M. Hauschild, Advancing water footprint assessments: Combining the impacts of water pollution and scarcity, Sci Total Environ 870 (2023) 161910.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Flumequine-mediated fluorescent zeolitic imidazolate framework functionalized by Eu(3+) for sensitive and selective detection of UO(2)(2+), Ni(2+) and Cu(2+) in nuclear wastewater
J. Hazard. Mater.

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: D. Mei, B. Yan, Flumequine-mediated fluorescent zeolitic imidazolate framework functionalized by Eu(3+) for sensitive and selective detection of UO(2)(2+), Ni(2+) and Cu(2+) in nuclear wastewater, J Hazard Mater 447 (2023) 130822.
